title: Developer Adoption Review
summary: Product signals from docs usage, support tickets, onboarding interviews, and pilot-team notes.

sources.title: Docs analytics
sources.body:
Quickstart completion rose from 44% to 58% after the copy refresh, and the first API call step now has fewer exits. The biggest remaining drop happens during local setup and credential configuration, where users bounce after seeing environment-specific errors.

Briefing relevance: This document is part of the authored synthetic source packet for the developer adoption case. The strongest signal for the requested briefing is: Quickstart completion is improving but setup remains a drop-off point. A secondary signal, which may or may not belong in the final recommendation depending on the user request, is: Environment drift, stale CLI versions, and credential recovery drive support volume.

Decision boundary: A good briefing should cite this document only for claims it directly supports. The main trap to avoid is: Do not claim pilot expansion is safe without addressing setup recovery. If this document is cited for a broader rollout, approval, or safety claim, the evaluator should check whether the body text actually contains that gate.

sources.title: Support digest
sources.body:
Most new tickets mention environment drift, stale CLI versions, and unclear recovery steps after failed installs. Several teams found the right command only after support pasted an internal checklist, which is not yet reflected in public docs.

Briefing relevance: This document is part of the authored synthetic source packet for the developer adoption case. The strongest signal for the requested briefing is: Environment drift, stale CLI versions, and credential recovery drive support volume. A secondary signal, which may or may not belong in the final recommendation depending on the user request, is: Senior reviewers require traceable recommendations tied to source snippets.

Decision boundary: A good briefing should cite this document only for claims it directly supports. The main trap to avoid is: Do not omit citation traceability when making product recommendations. If this document is cited for a broader rollout, approval, or safety claim, the evaluator should check whether the body text actually contains that gate.

sources.title: Pilot interviews
sources.body:
Senior engineers said they trust generated recommendations only when each claim points back to source snippets they can inspect. They were less concerned about prose polish than about knowing whether a recommendation came from analytics, support, or interview evidence.

Briefing relevance: This document is part of the authored synthetic source packet for the developer adoption case. The strongest signal for the requested briefing is: Senior reviewers require traceable recommendations tied to source snippets. A secondary signal, which may or may not belong in the final recommendation depending on the user request, is: Pilot expansion should wait for failed-install recovery and citation checks.

Decision boundary: A good briefing should cite this document only for claims it directly supports. The main trap to avoid is: Do not cite the analytics improvement as evidence that setup is solved. If this document is cited for a broader rollout, approval, or safety claim, the evaluator should check whether the body text actually contains that gate.

sources.title: Pilot expansion note
sources.body:
Two additional teams are available for a pilot next month, but the launch owner flagged setup recovery as the visible failure that would make the demo feel brittle. The note recommends expanding only after failed-install guidance and citation checks are observable in the lab.

Briefing relevance: This document is part of the authored synthetic source packet for the developer adoption case. The strongest signal for the requested briefing is: Pilot expansion should wait for failed-install recovery and citation checks. A secondary signal, which may or may not belong in the final recommendation depending on the user request, is: Quickstart completion is improving but setup remains a drop-off point.

Decision boundary: A good briefing should cite this document only for claims it directly supports. The main trap to avoid is: Do not claim pilot expansion is safe without addressing setup recovery. If this document is cited for a broader rollout, approval, or safety claim, the evaluator should check whether the body text actually contains that gate.

sources.title: Reviewer calibration document
sources.body:
Reviewers use this calibration document to decide whether the briefing selected the right evidence from the packet. The expected coverage points for this case are: Quickstart completion is improving but setup remains a drop-off point. Environment drift, stale CLI versions, and credential recovery drive support volume. Senior reviewers require traceable recommendations tied to source snippets. Pilot expansion should wait for failed-install recovery and citation checks.

The calibration note is intentionally not a model answer. It gives the evaluator enough context to distinguish direct support from plausible synthesis. A strong briefing should still rely on the source-specific operational notes above when making claims, and it should use this document only to understand how the packet will be judged.

The repeated failure modes under review are setup-recovery, citation-grounding. When a generated briefing cites sources broadly without connecting the claim to a concrete caveat, gate, or decision boundary, reviewers should mark the citation as weak even if the source is topically related.

sources.title: Risk and distractor document
sources.body:
This document records traps that make the packet realistic for eval work. Known traps include: Do not claim pilot expansion is safe without addressing setup recovery. Do not omit citation traceability when making product recommendations. Do not cite the analytics improvement as evidence that setup is solved.

Several details in the packet are deliberately adjacent to the user request but not sufficient for a shipping recommendation. For example, a metric improvement should not automatically become a rollout recommendation, a faster path should not automatically become the default path, and a draft or generated recommendation should not be treated as an approved action.

The intended task is: Summarize adoption risks and recommend the next product-quality improvement for pilot expansion. A good answer should prioritize the evidence that changes the recommendation, identify caveats that block overconfident claims, and leave open questions where the packet does not contain enough information.
